“I am who I am,” she says, pointing out that not only her gender but also doing an apprenticeship at Audi and studying law has shaped her perspective. “Maybe sometimes I am softer than a man. But when I was with the under-19s, a father [of one of the players] told me that the strength of a woman was something I should not lose. So I just try to be authentic to who I am, not to make myself extra hard or anything.”

Wittmann is equally sanguine about the haters. “I’ve had negative stuff on social media and in stadiums but I try not to focus on that stuff because if it comes down to conversations, nine out of 10 are really positive and one is negative. The loudest [voices] are sometimes the most negative ones but I try to focus on the nine that are positive.”

Wittmann’s commitment to Ingolstadt doesn’t mean she isn’t fiercely ambitious, knowing that an eventual progression would mean she and the club had enjoyed real success. She is prepared to dig in like Germany’s coach, Julian Nagelsmann, whom she greatly admires, another young coach wrongly perceived as an overnight success.

“It took me almost 15 years to coach a professional team,” Wittmann says. “It’s not just about the knowledge that you add to yourself but about your own confidence. Be authentic, stick to it and be patient.”

Patience as the watchword is what would appear to make Wittmann and Ingolstadt the ideal match.

News.title: Gas shortage fears as Middle East flow runs dry amid looming heating bill hikes
News.content:
BRITAIN reportedly has just two days’ worth of natural gas left in storage, sparking fears of a potential energy crunch as supplies from the Middle East run dry.

New figures from transmission operator National Gas show the UK’s reserves have plunged from 18,000 GWh last year to just 6,700 GWh – enough to cover just 1.5 days of demand.





A similar volume is currently held in tanks as liquefied natural gas.

In comparison, other European countries have built up reserves lasting several weeks and are better prepared to weather fluctuations in supply.

The supply squeeze has pushed traders to charge Britain a premium for gas, taking advantage of the country’s need to outbid competitors abroad.

As a result, the UK is now paying the highest wholesale gas prices in Europe.

It comes as Britain effectively operates hand-to-mouth, depending on a steady stream of tankers carrying American liquefied natural gas (LNG), along with pipeline gas from Norway, to keep the lights on and homes heated.

The UK used to have up to 12 days worth of gas in storage, but the system collapsed after successive government ministers pulled its funding.

National Gas data showed that gas stores were at 18 per cent of their former capacity on Friday, while LNG stores were just over half full.

A National Gas spokesman said the UK gets most of its gas from Norway and its own North Sea, reports The Telegraph.



The spokesman told the outlet: “The UK benefits from a wide range of gas supply sources. These provide the flexibility needed to balance supply and demand.”

But a report sent to ministers by National Gas warned about the growing shortage of storage as production in the North Sea begins to fall sharply.

In the report, National Gas chief executive Jon Butterworth warned Energy Secretary Ed Miliband that keeping Britain’s lights on would require either three new gas storage sites or six massive barges capable of processing LNG and feeding the gas into the UK network.

Mr Butterworth said ministers must “recognise the critical role gas continues to play in maintaining a stable and reliable energy system – particularly during periods of cold weather and low renewable generation”.

He added: “Declining North Sea production and recent changes in global energy markets are increasing Britain’s reliance on imports and tightening supply margins in the coming years.



“At the same time, a more turbulent geopolitical landscape and the increasing frequency of extreme weather events are introducing new risks to our energy security.”

Meanwhile, fears of a significant spike in oil prices are also growing, driven largely by disruption to the flow through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guard vowed to “set ablaze” any Western tanker attempting to sail through the strait – and hundreds have since amassed at either end.

Goldman Sachs warned that the current drop in the Middle East’s oil output is 17 times larger than the peak drop in Russia‘s output after it invaded Ukraine.

The bank said: “We now think that oil prices would likely exceed $100 next week if no signs of solutions emerge by then,” the bank said.



“We now also think it’s likely that oil prices, especially for refined products, would exceed the 2008 and 2022 peaks, if Strait of Hormuz flows were to remain depressed throughout March.”

It comes as Kemi Badenoch warned of gas storage shortages during her speech at the Conservative Party conference.

She said: “The UK only has enough gas storage to last for eight days. Mark my words, a price shock is coming.

“And when it does, it won’t just hit our pockets. It will have a huge impact on Britain’s borrowing costs too.”

Meanwhile, Prof Mohamed El-Erian of the University of Pennsylvania warned that households across the UK will be “hit from multiple sides” by price rises caused by the escalating conflict in the Middle East.



He told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme: “Once again, we see the UK more vulnerable to external shocks than otherwise that in turn is going to translate into higher mortgage rates.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“So the average person will get hit from multiple sides, unfortunately.”

“The average person is going to face higher energy prices, but also higher mortgage rates and slowly but surely, noticeable increases in a broad range of goods and services because of supply chain disruptions.”

News.title: Is Cyprus right to demand UK remove base from island after ‘failure to be a reliable ally’? VOTE NOW
News.content:
CYPRUS has called for British bases to be removed after last week’s drone strike on RAF Akrotiri.

Residents have protested the bases and said the UK territory makes the region “more of a target“.



“Say it loud, say it clear, British bases out of here” was chanted by around 200-300 demonstrators in the capital of Nicosia yesterday.



One local, Shona Muir, said: “I think that we’d be a lot safer without them here,” while holding up a British Bases Out banner.”

Cypriot foreign minister Constantinos Kombos has said: “I don’t think anyone anywhere in the world would be in any position of accepting the presence of bases on the island…given the clear concern, given the way things have moved forward.”

This comes as Reform UK Treasury spokesman: Robert Jenrick has blasted Sir Keir Starmer for his “failure to be a reliable ally” to the US.

He added that this has put the Special Relationship “under a lot of strain”.



As Starmer struggles to help Britain’s allies, is Cyprus right to want the UK out? Have your say in our exclusive poll.

Love to debate? Join Sun Club for just £1.99 a month for the boldest opinion on the issues shaping Britain.

News.title: Cocaine ‘super highways’ & bloodthirsty power grabs… How world’s most vicious cartels are becoming as powerful as states
News.content:
COCAINE “superhighways”, narco subs and international cartel alliances signal a new kind of organised crime powerful enough to take over states, experts warn.

Vicious Latin American drug gangs are evolving at an unprecedented pace – snatching territory and infiltrating governments to allow massive drug shipments across the Atlantic.





Now, their power and influence is growing – particularly as they join forces with Europe’s criminal networks, experts say.

Just last month, British cops helped seize a record nine-tonne haul of cocaine from a “narco sub” headed for Portugal from Latin America.

Meanwhile, government crackdowns are failing.



In Mexico, cartels control one third of the country – run by 400 separate mobs who carry high-powered rifles and execute enemies with no mercy.

And the recent killing of barbaric narco leader El Mencho sparked a vicious war as his criminal empire hit back.

The reclusive head of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), which were designated a terrorist organisation last year, had an £11.1million bounty on his head in the US.

A fresh-faced successor, the slain kingpin’s stepson, is already primed to take over the gang – showing they are still in power.



Criminal network specialist Rafaella Lipschitz, told The Sun the growing power of these groups creates new challenges that could “overwhelm traditional law enforcement”.

She told The Sun: “As their reach expands, criminals penetrate institutions in each state, especially in weaker states.”

Criminal gangs taking over governments and forcing ordinary citizens to serve their interests is becoming a bigger challenge, she says.

Lipschitz said this is “moving beyond simple corruption” to building new government networks – and effectively running regions themselves.

“This represents a shift from criminals operating against the state to criminals operating through the state,” she said.



“The line between legitimate authority and criminal power becomes blurred.”

In Mexico, cartels enforce their own services on civilians to push out the role of government in certain towns.

Ruthless groups have set up their own extortionate Wi-Fi and tax systems to rake in cash – with residents forced to comply or face execution.

Across Latin America and the Caribbean, drug cartels already wield a huge amount of power.

Cartels also function as employers, grooming poor youngsters into their brutal regimes.



In nearby Venezuela, a sprawling cocaine trafficking network is run by the very top officials – including tyrant Nicolas Maduro before he was apprehended by the US.

Cartel de los Soles, translated as Cartel of The Suns, was a powerful web of Venezuelan military, intelligence and political elites accused of taxing, protecting and moving cocaine using state resources.



And despite Maduro’s capture, many of these officials remain – allowing corruption and state-involvement in illegal dealings to continue.

Ecuador has also been infiltrated by gangs using threats and coercion to infiltrate its police, justice system, and local government.

Meanwhile, hundreds of armed groups have taken control of huge swathes of Haiti, particularly its capital Port-au-Prince.

Now these groups are moving across the Atlantic via “super highways” for cocaine trafficking – connecting South America’s coca-producing nations to Europe’s growing consumer markets.

As these criminal networks expand so will their influence – expanding their empire with new recruits, Lipschitz said.



Just months ago, French cops dismantled a major synthetic drug network with links to Mexico’s Sinaloa cartel.

The operation led to the seizure of more than 200kg of crystal meth and arrests of 16 people across Europe.

Investigators said the ringleaders relied on the support and expertise of Mexican cartel operatives.



In other cases, Mexican cartels have helped set up meth labs in countries like Belgium and the Netherlands.

Massive cocaine shipments passing through European ports such as Rotterdam and Antwerp have also been seized.

And criminals are diversifying their routes and finding new ways to move drug hauls at a rate law enforcement cannot “anticipate or detect”, Lipschitz said.

As they expand into Europe, so will their brutal violence, experts warn.

Organised crime-related violence has intensified, particularly in UK port cities, where control of supply chains and competition is intense.



The EU Drugs Agency has described seaports as the “floodgates” for illegal drugs, with growing concern over drug-related violence.

Last month, British cops helped seize a record nine-tonne haul of cocaine from a “narco sub” in the Atlantic Ocean.

The mammoth seizure, worth around £520million and weighing nearly as much as a school bus, was headed for Portugal from Latin America.



Cathy Haenlin, director of organised crime and policing studies at RUSI told The Sun: “In the UK, there is evidence that organised crime groups have infiltrated or exploited UK ports to move drugs and other illegal commodities into the country.”

In 2017, a gang managed to smuggle £10million worth of cocaine from Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, to the UK through Heathrow airport with the help of bent baggage handlers.

Handlers would transfer the bags on to the domestic reclaim belt for a courier arriving on an international flight to collect. The courier would then be able to pass through lower level security.

Gangs are increasingly operating from within private-sector and government structures.

Criminals have already corrupted Border Force officers, cruise ship workers, hauliers, couriers and airline baggage handlers.



With weakened governments infiltrated by drug gangs, criminal networks can swoop in and expand their operations, Lipschitz said.

Ruptures in state relationships reduce intelligence sharing, creating “blind spots” for criminal groups to cash in on.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Lipschitz said: “Breakdowns in US-UK partnerships due to the US’ unilateral targeting of alleged Venezuelan drug trafficking boats led the UK to stop some intelligence sharing with the US.

“This has left gaps for cartels and criminal networks to exploit, especially given that many of them operate on both sides of the Atlantic.”

Killing of Drug kingpin El Mencho sparks chaos THE death of feared drug lord “El Mencho” sparked a terrifying response by Mexico’s most vengeful cartel. Nemesio Rubén Oseguera Cervantes – leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (JNGC) – was killed on Sunday during a military operation in the western state of Jalisco. The JNGC responded with fire and fury, unleashing chaos that shut down streets and sent tourists fleeing. The cartel is one of Mexico’s most powerful and fastest-growing criminal organisations. Several hours of roadblocks on the streets of Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta saw vehicles set alight as gunmen in tactical gear forced civilians into hiding. Plumes of smoke rose over two major Mexican cities – Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta – where severe travel warnings were put in place. Authorities called in federal reinforcements to contain the violence. Local media reported cartel assaults in Jalisco, Michoacan, Tamaulipas, Colima, Guanajuato, Aguascalientes and Veracruz following Oseguera’s death. In February, the Trump administration designated the Jalisco New Generation Cartel as a foreign terrorist organisation. It has attacked the military, including helicopters, and pioneered the use of explosives launched from drones and the installation of mines.

News.title: Trump weighing up ‘commando raid’ on Iran’s nuke sites as desperate regime races to salvage uranium from bombed bunkers
News.content:
DONALD Trump is said to be weighing up a daring special forces raid on Iran’s shattered nuclear bunkers as the regime scrambles to recover uranium that could fuel a new atomic bomb.

US intelligence chiefs warn Tehran may still be able to reach highly enriched uranium buried beneath rubble at one of its key nuclear sites.





The move raises fears the fanatical regime could try to revive its nukes programme.

The material is believed to be trapped inside underground bunkers at Isfahan, which were smashed during last year’s joint US-Israeli bombing campaign.

Follow The Sun’s live blog for the latest updates on Iran

But officials now believe Iranian crews could reach the uranium through a narrow entrance leading into the wrecked complex.

Trump confirmed he is considering sending in elite American troops to seize the deadly stockpile before Iran can get its hands on it.

Speaking aboard Air Force One, the President said: “At some point, maybe we will. We haven’t gone after it. We wouldn’t do it now. Maybe we will do it later.

“If we ever did that, [the Iranians] would be so decimated that they wouldn’t be able to fight on the ground level.”



The uranium was effectively “entombed” beneath vast quantities of rubble after US and Israeli forces hammered Iran‘s nuclear facilities in Isfahan, Natanz and Fordow during the ferocious 12-day war.

Despite the devastation, intelligence officials believe the regime may still control around 450kg of uranium enriched to 60 per cent, The Telegraph reports.

Experts say it would take just weeks to convert the material to 90 per cent weapons-grade, enough to built up to 11 nuclear bombs.

Washington and Jerusalem are now weighing drastic options to stop that happening.

It comes as…



One possibility is a covert special forces operation to physically recover the uranium.

Another is sending in nuclear specialists to dilute the material on site, rendering it useless for weapons.

A US official told Axios: “The first question is, where is it? The second question is, how do we get to it and how do we get physical control?”

“And then, it would be a decision of the president and the department of war, CIA, as to whether we wanted to physically transport it or dilute it on premises.”

Another source said any mission would likely involve “small special ops raids – not a big force going in.”



Officials stress the operation is not imminent, with US and Israeli forces expected to further weaken Iran‘s military first through continuing airstrikes.

The US initially decided against retrieving the uranium after the war last June, when Trump concluded it would be too dangerous.

During the campaign – dubbed Operation Midnight Hammer – American B-2 stealth bombers and submarines unleashed a devastating barrage of bunker-buster bombs and Tomahawk cruise missiles.

The strikes obliterated key parts of Tehran’s nuclear programme, with Trump declaring the assault a “spectacular success” that “obliterated” the regime’s atomic ambitions.



But fresh intelligence now suggests the mullahs are desperately trying to salvage what survived.

Satellite images earlier this year showed Iran slapping roofs over bombed nuclear buildings at Natanz and Isfahan, in an apparent attempt to hide salvage work from spy satellites.

Security experts warned the structures could allow crews to quietly dig through the wreckage to recover enriched uranium or sensitive equipment.

The developments come as Trump and his top officials insist Iran will never be allowed to rebuild its nuclear arsenal.

Vice President JD Vance warned Tehran had already been caught trying to revive its bomb programme.



He said: “The principle is very simple: Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon.

“If they try to rebuild a nuclear weapon, that causes problems for us.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Vance added US intelligence had “seen evidence that [Iran] had tried to do exactly that.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio also warned the regime remains a grave threat, saying: “People are going to have to go and get it.”

News.title: Fears grow for missing Brazilian woman, 30, who vanished after phone sent ‘emergency ping’ from sea in UK coastal town
News.content:
FIND HER Fears grow for missing Brazilian woman, 30, who vanished after phone sent ‘emergency ping’ from sea in UK coastal town

FEARS are growing for a missing psychologist last seen five days ago near an Essex harbour as police admit they are “very concerned” for her wellbeing.

Cops and friends of Brazilian Vitoria Figueiredo Barreto have launched appeals for information about the 30-year-old who had been visiting Britain to investigate the possibility of starting a doctorate.





She is understood to have been staying with a female friend living in Colchester and maintained contact with a teacher at the University of Essex where she had reportedly been taking part in some research and academic training activities.

Pals have expressed fears after CCTV footage shows Vitoria getting off a bus and heading towards Brightlingsea Harbour in Essex.

Friend and fellow psychologist Fernanda Silvestre told Brazilian tabloid newspaper O Povo that Vitoria’s phone which is paired with hers had activated emergency mode and sent a signal location pointing to the sea.



She said: “Vitoria would go to university in the morning, they would have lunch, and she would take the bus to go sightseeing around London.

“So far they have searched hospitals, looked for people without documents, checked bank transactions, and are searching the surrounding area, but I haven’t seen this work being done at sea.”

Confirming Vitoria had been to the UK before and intended returning to Brazil in May for a conference, Fernanda added: “Vitoria speaks at least four languages, is very charismatic, and knows how to get by. She has travelled internationally several times.

Essex Police, who have been sharing photos of Vitoria and naming her using just her second surname as they urge anyone with information to call 999, said in a first appeal on Thursday: “We are looking for Vitoria Barreto who is missing from the Southend area.



“Vitoria is 30 years old and is white, of medium build and height and has long dark brown hair.

“She was last seen at the University of Essex campus in Wivenhoe, Colchester, on Tuesday. And we believe she boarded a bus heading to Brightlingsea the same day.

“She was last seen wearing a dark coat, a blue turtleneck jumper, light blue jeans and black trainers. She was carrying a white shoulder bag with the words ‘people over profit’.

“We’re worried about her and need to check she is ok.”

In an updated appeal on Friday alongside photos of the missing woman on the bus she boarded, the police force added: “Officers searching for Vitoria Barreto, who has been missing since Tuesday, are sharing some new images with the public today.



“Thirty-year-old Vitoria was last seen boarding a number 87 bus in Boundary Road at just after 1pm on Tuesday and got off 30 minutes later in Bellfield Avenue, Brightlingsea.

“We believe that later that same evening she may have been in the Brightlingsea Harbour area.

“We are working hard to locate Vitoria. We are very concerned and need to find her to make sure she is okay.



“Please take a look at these two new images of Vitoria as this is what she was last seen wearing.

“If you have seen her or have any information about where she might be then please call us immediately on 999.”

The missing psychologist, who hails from the city of Fortaleza in north-east Brazil, left her homeland in January to travel to Morocco for a conference before jetting to Britain.

Friends and well-wishers have been circulating posters with appeals for information about her on Facebook and Instagram.

Fernanda, in her own appeal, admitted she was feeling desperate in a selfie video message overnight as she said: “My friend Vitoria Figueiredo Barreto has disappeared near London.



“The last signal from her phone came from a small boat near the docks. Authorities have been contacted. If anyone has any information please contact us, and please share.

“We need the searches to be widened so Vitoria can be found alive. We’re talking about human life.”

She was quoted by another Fortaleza-based media outlet as saying she feared Vitoria could have been the victim of human trafficking.

Fernanda added: “Her mum is in London and they are taking the necessary steps but due to the seriousness of the situation, searches need to be carried out at sea and other European countries need to be alerted because from the clues we have, we realise this may be a case of human trafficking.”

Another Brazilian psychologist echoing the appeal, said: “We are issuing an urgent request for help.



“Vitoria has been missing in London since Tuesday.

“She was in the area around the University of Essex, near London. At this moment, her family is in London with friends.

“The local police and the Brazilian consulate have been notified, and the search is underway.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Essex Police are said to be searching the water near Brightlingsea Harbour.

The Sun has contacted the Foreign Office and the Brazilian Embassy for comment.

News.title: My son, 7, has been left terrified as the jobsworth council have decided to tear down his PLAY ROOM – it’s cost me £40k
News.content:
OUT OF PLAY My son, 7, has been left terrified as the jobsworth council have decided to tear down his PLAY ROOM – it’s cost me £40k

A SEVEN-year-old boy has been left terrified after a council decided to rip down his playroom – costing his family £40,000.

Dad Jon O’Brien paid the hefty sum for an architect to build his son a “safe haven” games room at their home in Acton, West London.





Just two years earlier, a 23-year-old man had been shot dead near the property.

Wanting to avoid his son having concerns about his safety, Jon built the room for his son and pals to have a safe place to play away from the dangerous streets.

But Ealing Council now wants to tear it down.

Shortly after the rooftop chalet was complete, a local landlord lodged a complaint – leading to a visit from council officials who told him he didn’t have planning permission.

Jon paid a top architect to build the room who had an “incredible” portfolio, including projects for footballers.

But it soon emerged the architect hadn’t bothered to sort planning permission, sending the future of the play room into crisis.

Jon told The Sun: “I was absolutely gutted. I couldn’t believe after all this time and numerous conversations, he didn’t do it! It’s all weird to me, why can’t you just do things by the book?”



Setting him back £40,000, his entire life savings at the time, Ealing Council’s plans to scupper his “safe haven” were devastating especially for his seven-year-old son Del.

When he explained to his lad that the planning inspector would be visiting, Del became “scared of the government” and convinced they’re “bad people”.

When he pleaded to keep the room, his dad told him: “No, no, no, they won’t let us keep it.”

With only 10 windows overlooking the games room, Jon is suspicious as to why the council sent out over 130 letters asking for local’s opinions.

He claims many of the letters were sent to an entirely different block of flats on the other side of the road.



He added: “They sent out something like 130 letters to everyone’s address to try and get everyone on board which was a bit of a sneaky tactic because people hadn’t complained, they were trying to encourage people to.”

“It seemed like they were looking for an issue to justify what they were doing.”

The complaint was submitted by landlord David Jenkins who owns a property overlooking the wooden games room.



But Jon claims the tenants see no problem with the structure and their children regularly play in the “safe haven”.

He continued: “Apparently he was round there every day watching it get built.

“I’m just a normal bloke, he could’ve come and spoke to me and said ‘what are you doing?

“It just seems like a bit of bitterness and a bit of jealousy.

“The fact he’d let it get built, then take photos and make a complaint, is this what the world’s come to?



“The fact you’ve got to do it like that rather than just be a friendly neighbour.”

In response Mr Jenkins told The Sun: “It is an eyesore outside my flat, it was built without planning permission. What if it slides down off his roof in a storm and kills someone?

“How dare someone decide to just build a wooden shed on top of their house without asking any kind of permission or expect to meet any standards.

“This is the sort of thing that planning permission was created to prevent.”

The fiasco has put a big strain on Jon’s family life, forcing them to consider moving out of London after 10 years living in the city.



He said: “It caused us no end of hassle at home and arguing between me and my wife, it’s definitely aged us.”

Now he is appealing for legal property advisors to get in touch so his family can consider their next steps.

An Ealing Council spokesperson said: “The Planning Inspectorate has dismissed the owners planning appeal and upheld the council’s enforcement notice.

“The inspector found the unauthorised roof extension and staircase caused unacceptable harm to neighbouring residents, including loss of light, outlook and privacy, and conflicted with planning policy.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“While we understand the personal circumstances put forward, the Inspector was clear that these did not outweigh the planning harm identified.



“Planning decisions must be made in accordance with planning legislation.”

News.title: Woman, 28, arrested after man found dead in train station car park in early hours
News.content:
MURDER PROBE Woman, 28, arrested after man found dead in train station car park in early hours

Credit: Google Streetview

A WOMAN has been arrested after a man was found dead in a train station car park.

The 28-year-old male was found at Tamworth station car park in the early hours of this morning.



Officers from Staffordshire Police were called to Victoria Road at around 2.20am following reports of a man being found unresponsive.



The man was treated by paramedics but sadly confirmed dead at the scene.

A 28-year-old woman was arrested and remains in custody.

Cops have been carrying out a forensic examination of the scene.

Due to the location, British Transport Police has now taken the lead in the investigation.



Senior Investigating Officer, Detective Inspector Marvin Bruno said: “Our specialist family liaison officers are providing support to the victim’s family and our thoughts remain with them at this time.

“We are urging anyone with any information or anyone who witnessed events leading up to the man’s death to get in touch with us.”

Anyone with information is urged to contact British Transport Police on 0800 40 50 40 or by texting 61016 quoting 60 of 8 March.

Crimestoppers can also be contacted anonymously on 0800 555 111.

News.title: Man found dead outside Glasgow’s controversial drugs ‘fix room’
News.content:
A MAN has died outside Glasgow’s controversial drugs “fix room”.

Emergency services were called to the area outside the city’s drug consumption hub The Thistle in Hunter Street at around 10.20am today.





Police confirmed the man was found dead near to the centre in the east end.

Officers say the death is not being treated as suspicious but enquiries remain ongoing.

A Police Scotland spokesperson said: “Around 10.20am on Sunday, 8 March, 2026, we received a report of a death of a man outdoors in the Hunter Street area of Glasgow.

“Enquiries are ongoing however there do not appear to be any suspicious circumstances. A report will be submitted to the Procurator Fiscal.”

The death comes amid growing concerns surrounding the centre – which is the only one of its kind in the UK.

The ‘fix room’ allows addicts to inject substances such as heroin and cocaine under medical supervision – at an eye-watering cost of £362 per hit.

Critics say the £2.3million-a-year centre has brought more drug activity to the surrounding streets and left residents fearing for their safety as dealers have moved in to target addicts.

Figures show between January and October last year, users had taken 4,767 heroin and cocaine injections at the site.



In that time 367 men and 94 women made 7,165 visits — working out at around £3,742 per user.

We told last year how staff at the centre, which has eight injecting booths, had recorded 60 medical emergencies, including 11 ambulance call-outs in 10 months of the centre opening.

Official statistics show cocaine featured in nearly 70 per cent of injecting episodes, with heroin used in around a fifth. A lethal mix of both drugs — known as a “snowball” — accounted for 10 per cent.

Locals have also complained of drug users taking substances in nearby streets and leaving dirty needles behind.

One woman said: “People were advised that there would be less drug traffic by drug users, there’d be less discarded needles within the streets, and the areas would actually improve.



“It’s the total opposite. There’s more drug traffic, more crime. Numerous residents have actually purchased baseball bats because they’re feeling unsafe.”

Campaigners have blasted the huge spending on the facility and say the cash would be better spent on rehab programmes.

Annemarie Ward, of charity Faces and Voices of Recovery, said: “It’s just incredible. It’s utter insanity — an obscene ethical and moral failure.

“If this is compassion, it’s the most expensive form of neglect Scotland has ever devised.

“Glasgow’s drug room costs more to keep people unwell than it would to help people recover.



“For the cost of keeping one person injecting for a year, Scotland could fund five or even six full-time rehab programmes that actually save lives.

“I don’t understand why we are prolonging people’s addiction.

“Why are we spending so much on helping people use drugs?”

The centre has also come under fire after it emerged last year that not a single addict using the facility had been referred to rehab in the first 10 months of it opening.

Despite the criticism, officials say the hub provides a vital contact point for people battling addiction.



A Glasgow Health and Social Care Partnership spokesman said: “The Thistle provides a critical first point of contact for people who may never have otherwise entered a health or treatment service before.

“These are the same individuals who would otherwise be injecting in alleyways, stairwells, and public spaces, all completely unsupported and far more exposed to the risk of overdose and death. People do not transition from long-term drug dependence into rehab overnight.”

Scotland remains the drug deaths capital of Europe, despite figures showing fatalities fell to 1,017 in 2024, down by 155 from the previous year.

Earlier figures also revealed 133 deaths in the first half of 2025 — the same as the year before.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

Experts warn it could take until 2029 before it is clear whether the controversial facility has reduced drug-related harm.

But despite the ongoing debate, plans are already in place to open a second consumption room in Edinburgh’s Old Town.

News.title: 'I sat through Ian Huntley's entire trial and I have one main recollection of the monster'
News.content:
'I sat through Ian Huntley's entire trial and I have one main recollection of the monster'

Gordon Rayner, who sat through every day of Ian Huntley's trial has written about one major recollection from the chilling courtroom scene

View 3 Images Ian Huntley (Image: Ian Stenning/Daily Mirror)

A man who attended every single day of child killer Ian Huntley's trial has shared a lasting memory of the murderer, who died earlier this week.

‌



Huntley, a former school caretaker, was convicted and jailed for the murders of two 10-year-old girls, Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman, in Soham, Cambridgeshire on August 4, 2002. The girls' bodies were discovered in a ditch roughly 10 miles from Soham following a 13 day search, in a case that sent shockwaves across the UK.

‌



News of Huntley's death was announced over the weekend, around a week after he was attacked at the maximum security prison where he was being held.

‌



In the aftermath of his death, more people have come forward to share their recollections of encounters with Huntley, both prior to his arrest and throughout his time on trial.

Among those recounting their experiences is Gordon Rayner, who was present in the courtroom throughout the entire trial, seated just feet away from the killer, reports the Express.

View 3 Images Ian Huntley (Image: Ian Stenning/Daily Mirror)

‌



Writing in the Telegraph, Mr Rayner recalled how Huntley nitpicked over minor details even after admitting guilt.

He said: "One of my abiding recollections of the trial is that although he admitted the girls had died at his hands, Huntley nitpicked over facts that made no difference to his guilt.

"When the jury was told he had changed the tyres on his car after disposing of the bodies (so they did not match the tread pattern found at the scene). He insisted it was something he did once a year as a matter of course and had nothing to do with dumping the bodies."

‌



Mr Rayner added that Huntley even went so far as to dispute the depth of water in his bath where Holly was allegedly drowned.

View 3 Images Ian Huntley was one of Britain's most reviled murderers (Image: PA)

Following his trial, Huntley received two life sentences with a minimum term of 40 years to be served for the murders. He wasn't given a whole life tariff because the Criminal Justice Act 2003 was passed just a single day after his conviction.

‌



Earlier this year it was reported that Huntley had been assaulted at HMP Frankland, the category A facility where he was imprisoned, before being rushed to hospital with severe brain trauma. On Friday he was reportedly taken off from life support.

In a statement following Huntley's death, a spokesperson for Durham Constabulary said: "A man who was attacked at HMP Frankland in Durham last week has died in hospital this morning.

"Ian Huntley, 52, was taken to hospital with serious injuries following an incident in the workshop on the morning of Thursday 26 February.

Article continues below

"A police investigation into the circumstances of the incident is ongoing. A file is being prepared for the Crown Prosecution Service for consideration for charges."

News.title: Ian Huntley update as inmates had 'chilling talk night before skull was crushed'
News.content:
Ian Huntley update as inmates had 'chilling talk night before skull was crushed'

Soham murderer Ian Huntley was left brain damaged after a gruesome attack at 'Monster Mansion' and now prison sources have given a chilling update on his final days

View 3 Images The double child killer has died nine days after being attacked (Image: Andy Stenning/Daily Mirror)

When prison officers found Ian Huntley lying in a pool of his own blood, they assumed he was dead.

The killer, who left the nation reeling when he murdered two happy young girls in the quiet town of Soham, has died aged 52, nine days after a frenzied attack inside HMP Frankland, otherwise known as 'Monster Mansion'.

Surrounded by two armed officers and bleeping machines, he was severely brain damaged when he drew his last breaths on Saturday morning, with even his mother struggling to recognise him.

Huntley's skull was crushed with a metal pole at a prison workshop in a brutal beating, allegedly at the hands of a rapist triple killer. His wounds included brain injuries, skull fractures and a broken jaw.

"The attack was unbelievably savage – more savage than has so far been reported. It is astonishing that Huntley is still alive," a source said before his death. "He was bending down tying some string onto a recycling crate when he was first hit – so was in a position like you'd be in when tying your shoelaces."

View 3 Images The killer's skull was crushed in the attack (Image: Shutterstock)

One woman visiting a prisoner housed alongside Huntley told the Sun that the attack saw him "ripped apart like a rat". She added: "He's in a bad, bad way. I shouldn't say it, but it's what he deserves."

Medics 'performed miracles' to try and stablise the killer after the incident, and he was then placed in a medically induced coma in hospital and put on constant watch. A decision was made to cut off his life support machine on Friday night after a consultation with his mother Lydna Richards, and he died the following day. She is said to have told friends before his death that 'it's better if he doesn't pull through'.

Anthony Russell, the lag suspected to have delivered the brutal beating, reportedly shouted 'I've done it!' to other inmates as he was taken away in cuffs. On Saturday, cheers were heard echoing through the top security prison as news of Huntley's death spread around the cells.

Huntley was working in the segregated wing when he was smashed in the head with a metal bar produced from the recycling. In a chilling update, sources told The Sun that his inmates knew "the attack was coming" and had discussed it the night before.

View 3 Images HMP Frankland houses some of the most dangerous criminals (Image: ITV)

"When it first happened, he was totally unresponsive and could not breathe," another source said. "It was absolute chaos, and Huntley was in a terrible state. I'm amazed someone was able to get anywhere near him because he is usually really closely protected. They must have timed it when he wasn't with prison officers and must have used a weapon to injure him so severely."

Huntley's fellow inmates aren't the only ones not in mourning. The killer's daughter, Samantha Bryan, has expressed a profound sense of relief and said yesterday: "We should flush his ashes down the toilet." The beautician believes her beastly father does not deserve a funeral or a grave as he is going to "burn in hell".

Technically speaking, the decision to turn off Huntley's ventilator was meant to have been handed to Samantha. But as she never met him, and sees her father only as a monster, the burden appears to have fallen on his mother. Sources told The Sun that the decision was taken at around Friday lunchtime after brain tests revealed his condition. They also claimed his mum was by his bedside.

A source said: "This is it, this is the end of Huntley. He is effectively dead and, at the best, is drawing his last breaths. No one who has dealt with him is shedding a tear. Even his mother has accepted that this is for the best, having seen him and knowing what a state he is in."

Now, prison sources have reportedly the Daily Mail that the issue of Huntley's next of kin sparked a family 'disagreement'.

As former Met superintendent Nusrit Mehab puts it to The Mirror, the formal proceedings after a prisoner's demise can be somewhat complicated - depending on what their wishes were - if any - before their death.

"If his family are the legal guardians, they can claim it. If he's put somebody else in as a representative, or made a will, then they become the legal claimants, and if nobody comes forward, then it falls on the public health authority to do it," she explained.

Article continues below

The latter option seems to be true for the double child killer, as no-one wants to claim him. The 52-year-old is now expected to be cremated at a secret location in a £3,000 service funded by the state.

News.title: Final day at Crufts in photos as world's largest dog show delights
News.content:
The final day of Crufts is underway - with the much-coveted Best in Show trophy set to be awarded later.

More than 18,000 dogs have been competing at this year’s show at Birmingham's National Exhibition Centre since Thursday, with competitions including agility, flyball, freestyle and heelwork to music.

Events set to go ahead this evening include the West Midlands Police dog display, the Humanitarian Action of the Year Award, before the awarding of Best in Show and final presentation.

The winner of Crufts Best in Show receives a £200 cash prize and a replica of the Crufts Keddall Memorial Trophy, named after show manager Robert Keddall.

Over 500 trade stands have also been flogging their wares during the four-day event, which is the largest of its kind in the world.

Brave Mirror dog Betty even had a go at the Crufts agility course, with very mixed results.

Coverage of the world famous dog show began on Channel 4 on Thursday, with new TV host Claudia Winkleman describing it as "the best day of my life".

She said of the event: "I quite liked my wedding - this is better."

News.title: New UK dog rules 'could see 67 breeds banned' campaigners say - full list
News.content:
New UK dog rules 'could see 67 breeds banned' campaigners say - full list

A Parliamentary group is working on a 10-point checklist to determine if dogs are allowed

View 4 Images Corgis will face tough rules to make sure they are healthy

Campaigners say 67 breeds of dogs could be banned in the UK if new rules come into force. An all-party parliamentary group for animal welfare (APGAW) is working on new legislation to improve the treatment of dogs in the UK.

‌



The rules could see a ban on characteristics such as short noses, flat faces and shortened legs, reports The Times. The rules could use a 10-point checklist to determine if a breed is healthy. Banned features could also include mottled skin, excessive skin folds, bulging eyes, drooping eyelids, underbite, overbite and disrupted breathing.

‌



Critics of the rules say they would mean a ban on corgis, dachshunds, Westies, chihuahuas and shih tzus. The rules on short legs have been included because breeds like corgis and dachshunds can experience spine and leg deformities. Flat-face breeds are included due to concerns about multiple health problems for dogs that struggle to breathe.

‌



View 4 Images Chihuahuas could fall foul of the rule on bulging eyes

Beverley Cuddy, founder of the Union of Good Dog People, said: “It’s going to be the most shocking thing to happen to dog lovers in this country. People will rightly be outraged at being told they can’t have the dogs they want. It’s madness.”

Marisa Heath, director of the APGAW, said: “The suggestion that the Innate Health Assessment [IHA] could lead to beloved breeds such as corgis becoming 'banned' is misleading and irresponsible'.

‌



“The IHA is a voluntary welfare tool designed to encourage breeding from dogs with healthy conformations. It does not target particular breeds and is not intended to make any breed extinct — quite the opposite as it is about making those breeds healthier and sustainable by moving them towards good innate health. Indeed, many breeds cited as at risk — including corgis and dachshunds — have already passed the assessment in practice."

View 4 Images Australian Cattle Dogs are on te list campaigners say could be banned

While the tool is currently voluntary, it is already being used by some councils to judge if breeders are producing healthy dogs, and could become law within five years.

‌



Margaret Hoggarth, secretary of the Welsh Corgi League, said: “They are trying to say any dogs with a certain look are unhealthy but anybody who has worked with corgis knows they are a very healthy breed. They don’t have the problems you see with some other breeds but sadly they are being lumped in together because they have short legs.”

View 4 Images Shih Tzu dogs may fall foul of the new rules

Full list of breeds campaigners fear would be banned

1. Affenpischer

‌



2. American Cocker Spaniel

3. Australian Cattle Dog

4. Australian Shepherd

‌



5. Basset

6. Basset Bleu

7. Basset Fauve

‌



8. Beagle

9. Beauceron

10. Bergamasco

‌



11. Bloodhound

12. Border Collie

13. Boston Terrier

‌



14. Boxer

15. Bracco

16. Brittany

‌



17. Brussels Griffon

18. Bull Mastiff

19. Bulldog

‌



20. Cairn Terrier

21. Cardigan Welsh Corgi

22. Cavalier King Charles Spaniel

‌



23. Cesky Terrier

24. Chihuahua

25. Chow Chow

‌



26. Clumber Spaniel

27. Dachshund

28. Dandie Terrier

‌



29. Dogue de Bordeaux

30. French Bulldog

31. Glen of Imaal Terrier

‌



32. Grand Basset

33. Great Dane

34. Japanese Chin

‌



35. King Charles Spaniel

News.title: 'My day on NHS frontline showed me why pharmacists are key to saving the service'
News.content:
'My day on NHS frontline showed me why pharmacists are key to saving the service'

Mirror Health Editor Martin Bagot spends the day at a pharmacy to witness how Labour’s generational shift moving NHS care out of hospitals and onto the High Street is working

Pharmacist: ‘We’re the new ‘front door’ of the NHS - but we could be doing so much more”

It has been a conundrum that has bedevilled governments for almost two decades - how to keep more people well so they don’t turn up in A&E.

‌



I spent the day observing the new "front door" of the NHS which Health Secretary Wes Streeting is hoping will finally achieve a major shift in the way it delivers care. Pharmacists are on the front line of Labour’s vision for the future of the NHS - moving more hospital care into local communities.

‌



Mr Streeting has stated that much of NHS care that is currently done in hospitals will be done on the High Street within a decade, in chemists as well as local health centers. We witnessed how this transformation is working in practice, shadowing Naomi Haldane who told us how an army of pharmacists are now trained to prescribe drugs on behalf of the NHS - but are limited in what they are allowed to do.

‌



View 3 Images Naomi Haldane has been serving her community for 25 years and has seen her role change (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

NHS Pharmacy First Q&A as patients told to see chemist before GP for seven illnesses READ MORE :

Naomi has been a pharmacist at Boots in St Albans for 25 years and is a pillar of the local community, having served multiple generations of families and been invited to some patients’ funerals. She has seen how the role has changed over time.

She told the Mirror: “I feel we’re the front door of the NHS now because we’re accessible. People can just walk in to see us but many don’t realise they don’t need to make a GP appointment. There’s a lot more that we could do.”

‌



Sitting in a pharmacy for any length of time you realise how they have become vital community hubs and almost a drop-in GP service for minor ailments.

People came in with issues including problems sleeping, constipation as well as colds and flu. High street pharmacies are usually equipped with rooms for private consultations and staff are proactive in offering tests, checks and advice to keep people well.

Naomi said: “Yesterday I had someone in her 30s in for a contraceptive and we offered her a free NHS blood pressure test. It turned out that if it was just a few points higher I would have sent her immediately to A&E. People can be quite complacent about their health but high blood pressure is known as the ‘silent killer’ for a reason. It is common for people with it to have no symptoms.”

‌



While visiting the pharmacy I underwent the check and at the age of 42 I was surprised to be told that my first blood pressure reading was high.

After the photographer had stopped taking photos - and some deep breathing - it came down to just within the safe range. But it’s certainly something I will now monitor.

Historically the job of a pharmacist was predominantly to check that medicines packs are correct and the right dose before they were sent out to patients.

‌



However, the role has changed as much of this work is now automated, so the modern pharmacist now does a lot of patient consultations traditionally associated with a family doctor.

View 3 Images Patients can now get some prescription medicines without a GP appointment (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

The Pharmacy First scheme means pharmacists in England are authorised to supply prescription-only medicines such as antibiotics and antivirals for seven common conditions without a GP appointment.

‌



These are sinusitis, sore throat, earache, infected insect bite, the bacterial skin infection impetigo, shingles, and uncomplicated urinary tract infections (UTIs) in women.

Naomi said: “People may not realise they don’t need a GP appointment for a wide variety of conditions now. They are able to just walk in and see their pharmacist and there are so many conditions that we can now treat… our capabilities have been expanded. I had a woman in her 70s come in for sinusitis yesterday and she had contacted her GP first who had referred her. She was a retired pharmacist as well but she wasn’t aware she didn’t need to do that. She could have just come straight here.”

But pharmacists could do so much more for the NHS to take pressure off overstretched GPs. Many, like Naomi, have taken additional qualifications so they can legally prescribe drugs for any condition. All newly qualified pharmacists can also now legally prescribe medicines.

‌



However, they are not yet authorised to do so on the NHS outside of a limited number of select schemes, such as Pharmacy First. They mainly prescribe drugs privately.

Naomi said: “With all these prescribers there is now a whole body of people who could do a whole lot more. I can prescribe and all the pharmacists coming out of university will be prescribers as well. We’re ideally placed to take on what the NHS is going to give us. I feel there’s a skillset here. There are a lot of people who come into the store that I know what they need but I have to refer them to a GP.”

Critical NHS Transformation

The NHS shift from hospitals to the community has been tried before and has been an aspiration since a landmark 2008 review of the health service by Lord Ara Darzi.

‌



Shifting more care to primary care settings such as GPs and pharmacies was a key recommendation - because nipping ailments in the bud early before someone has to turn up in A&E is also cheaper for the NHS.

However David Cameron ’s Conservative Party came to power from 2010 and implemented a record NHS funding squeeze. Austerity policies meant all NHS services - primary and hospital - got steadily worse, resulting in more people turning up in A&E.

Despite a national aspiration to treat more people preventatively in the community, the NHS system kept diverting more of its funding towards hospitals to avert a growing A&E crisis that was most clearly costing lives.

‌



The more funding it diverted to A&Es meant there was less that the NHS could spend on primary care keeping people out of hospital - resulting in ever worsening pressures on A&Es that continue to this day.

All this came at the same time as an exodus of senior GPs and increasingly unmanageable workloads for those who remained in the NHS. It became harder and harder to get a GP appointment.

Since coming to power in 2024 Labour has pledged to finally tackle this seemingly intractable problem and move more NHS care away from hospitals and into the community.

‌



One way it is doing this is commissioning pharmacists to do more NHS work, to keep people well and out of hospital. But it remains to be seen whether the Government will finally achieve this elusive goal.

View 3 Images Stephanie Murphy getting her blood pressure checked (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

Stephanie Murphy, 67, from St Albans, popped in while out on a shopping outing with two friends. She said: “We came in for blister plasters and that young man asked if we wanted our blood pressure checked. I didn’t know they did that.” Pal Sue Giles, also 67, added: “It takes the pressure off the doctors, I suppose.”

‌



However if people are diagnosed with high blood pressure they are once more bounced back to their GP. Naomi explained: “Again, when we can see the reading is pretty high, we know the sort of blood pressure tablets they need. They have to go back to their GP but we know what they’ll be prescribed by the time they hopefully come back to us.”

Pharmacies also started offering women the ‘morning-after pill’ in England free from last year - removing the need for an awkward GP appointment. Oral contraceptives can also be prescribed by pharmacists.

Naomi said: “Previously that was done by the GP. Women really didn’t like that. It’s really quite a simple consultation to continue on a contraceptive but women had to struggle to get a GP appointment. Now they can just do it on their lunch break."

‌



Despite the stated NHS shift towards pharmacies, the sector has warned that underfunding puts this ambition at risk. The Mirror is campaigning to save family chemists and stop the hundreds of closures which are piling pressure on overstretched GPs.

The National Pharmacy Association has warned that smaller community pharmacies in particular face a financial “cliff edge” of rising costs from next month including higher business rates. Chief executive Henry Gregg said: "The Mirror is absolutely right to showcase and applaud the work of community pharmacies. This story is repeated day in day out in thousands of community pharmacies up and down the length of the land.

"However, too many pharmacies have been left hanging on by their fingertips due to the impact of historic squeezes to their funding, with record numbers shutting in the last decade and many others raiding personal savings to simply keep the lights on. It's clear they have huge untapped potential to take further pressure off the rest of the health system, including expanding prescribing services to treat more ailments, but only a sustained and long term increase to their funding will make this possible."

Article continues below

The Government told the Mirror that with four in every five people living within a mile from a pharmacy “they are a vital front door to the NHS”. A spokesman for the Department of Health and Social Care spokesperson said: “Pharmacies are central to our shift towards community-based care.

"We have increased community pharmacy funding to £3.1 billion which is the largest boost for any part of the NHS over the last two years. Our 10 Year Health Plan sets out how community pharmacies will play a central role in delivering the Neighbourhood Health Service and ensuring more people can receive care closer to home, freeing up GP appointments.”

News.title: 'I'm a bathroom expert - here are 5 common mistakes you're making (and how to fix them on a budget)'
News.content:
'I'm a bathroom expert - here are 5 common mistakes you're making (and how to fix them on a budget)'

Our bathroom is one of the most important spaces in our home, but we often forget about it, says design expert Andrea Clewett. She shares how to turn it into a sanctuary for less

View 5 Images Bathrooms are often small spaces and can feel cramped, while inadequate ventilation leads to damp and mildew

The bathroom is one of the most important spaces in a home, where we start the day and finish it, winding down for bed. It needs to be energising, with space for bottles and beauty products, well ventilated so that it smells fresh, and featuring colours that help us feel happy and grounded.

Yet most UK bathrooms fall short of this - they’re often small spaces and can feel cramped, while inadequate ventilation leads to damp and mildew. But it doesn’t have to be this way, says design expert Andrea Clewett, co-founder of custom printed bathroom shower panels brand Showerscape, which has worked with artists, hotels and residential properties.

She says that the humble room once used purely for freshening up is set to take centre stage as a home sanctuary - and there are easy ways we can all give our bathroom a new, stylish look without spending a fortune. 'It’s not about splashing out on everything,” she says.

“Small changes can have a big impact. You want to make your bathroom feel high-quality, as that’s what makes it feel luxurious without the huge price tag.” Here Andrea highlights where you’re going wrong - and how to transform your bathroom into a space you won’t want to leave.

Mistake...YOU ONLY HAVE ONE LIGHT

One of the biggest bathroom no-nos (after an avocado suite) is a single overhead light, casting its stark glare across the room. “Don’t panic, you don’t have to rewire the whole room, with some simple lighting tricks you can spread a softer, warmer glow around the room,” says Andrea.

View 5 Images LED strip lights provide an extra source of illumination over a mirror and in any coves and can come in several styles - they’re not the strip lights from your childhood school toilets (Image: Oscar Wong via Getty Images)

Opt for an LED illuminated bathroom cabinet to provide more atmospheric low lighting when you want it and some extra oomph when you need bright light for applying makeup or shaving.

LED strip lights provide an extra source of illumination over a mirror and in any coves and can come in several styles - they’re not the strip lights from your childhood school toilets.

Mistake... IT FEELS SMALL AND POKEY

Few people are lucky enough to have a large bathroom, so most of us have to be clever with the space that we have. “That means, not cluttering up surfaces and shelves with toiletries, having cleaning supplies taking up floor space and towels draped over everything,” says Andrea.

“Opaque shower curtains and frosted doors also shrink the room by blocking your view of the shower space beyond. And small tiles and lots of lines of grout make the walls look cluttered.”

Try choosing large tiles to create a more seamless surface or opt for floor-to-ceiling features such as a photographic panel in your shower to draw the eye upwards, making the room seem taller. Try a nature scene, a forest, a tranquil beach, or an antique bird print.

View 5 Images A photographic panel in your shower will draw the eye upwards

“Using large prints in a small room can also make the room feel more expansive and less cluttered, and it gives the illusion of space and depth,” says Andrea. "A large mirror can also almost double your perceived sense of space, reflecting the wall or feature opposite, or bouncing light from a window back into the room.

Mistake... IT’S ALL WHITE

All too often bathrooms look more like an abattoir than a much-loved, much-used household space, with white tiles, white bath, sinks and toilets. Not only will an all-white scheme make the room look anonymous and unloved, but it’ll seem cold and stark, even if the rest of your house is warm and inviting.

Andrea says: “People forget the bathroom is the first place you see in the morning and often the last one you’re in before bed. It should feel like a little sanctuary, not a clinical white box.” One of the biggest bathroom trends for 2026 is personalisation - turning them into zones of luxury, relaxation and personal expression.

So that means choosing the features, colours and textures that are right for you. A statement shower panel instantly creates a one-of-a-kind appearance. Choose a work of art like an abstract watercolour, or run an eye-catching peacock print behind your bath.

View 5 Images Peacock print shower mural, £474, Showerscape

Mistake... IT'S CLUTTERED

Lots of clutter makes a bathroom feel smaller, it also makes using anything slightly stressful as bottles teeter and topple on the edge of the bath or around the sink every time you move. So look for units that double up as storage, such as under-sink cabinets. Andrea says, “You don’t have to stick to a traditional bathroom unit. We used a three-drawer bedside cabinet from Dunelm in a small Welsh cottage. Make sure you seal around the sink with silicone and oil the unit’s surface regularly. It’s practical, looks great and gives you loads of storage. Likewise, old sideboards or cabinets can be transformed into chic vanities. It’s all about creativity, not cost.”

Mistake... YOU DON'T HAVE ANY ACCESSORIES

To really give your bathroom personality and detail that make you want to stay in there as long as possible, choose items that really make it a one-of-a-kind space. “Changing your accessories in the room can make a huge difference to the look and feel of the space,” says Andrea.

View 5 Images Your candle jars can be reused once they've burnt out (Image: Getty Images)

A few well placed candles will make your bathroom feel like a spa - one of the big bathroom trends for 2026. “Plants are amazing in a humid environment too. Boston ferns, spider plants, even small palms can make a bathroom feel like a tropical retreat,” says Andrea.

Swap old door handles for new brass, matte black or chrome ones. And add a statement soap dispenser so the sink area looks clean and luxe.

Article continues below

*For more information, visit showerscape.co.uk

News.title: People baffled by bizarre bathroom decoration choice in £600k house
News.content:
People baffled by bizarre bathroom decoration choice in £600k house

The house is on the market for £600,000, and features four bedrooms, three bathrooms, a garage, an electric vehicle charging point, a separate garden room, and even its own sauna

View Image People were baffled by what was inside the bathroom (stock image) (Image: Getty Images/iStockphoto)

People have been baffled by a bizarre design choice in one of the bathrooms of a house on the market for £600,000. Pictures featured in the Rightmove listing for the four-bedroom detached house in Skelton, just outside York, show a large living room and huge separate kitchen/diner area, a big garden, and a separate garden room.

‌



It also has three bathrooms, a garage, three bathrooms, an electric vehicle charging point, and even its own sauna room. But it's the design of one of the bathrooms that has people taken aback.

‌



The images, which appear to be of a smaller WC on the ground floor, show the walls of the room covered in what looks like artificial grass, or AstroTurf. The strange décor even features small yellow flowers dotted around the wall. And the floor is strange too - painted a deep blue, it features an image of four fish swimming towards the toilet.

‌



People on Reddit were baffled. One said: "Astroturf a wall? Why why why." Another wrote: "That’s the worst crazy golf hole I’ve ever seen."

Someone else replied: "It's rare that someone digs out a property photo of something that I've really, truly never seen before. Lots of weird stuff, sure, but usually variations on a theme. Horrifying AstroTurf Bathroom is the first truly new and unique one of the year for me."

Another wrote: "That is not a hygienic bathroom surface material." A different user said: "Did someone lose a bet? And HOW DO YOU CLEAN THIS?? Ewww." And someone else wrote: "Why why why, the rest of the house is so normal and nice?!"

‌



But others defended the choice. One said: "The downstairs loo reminds me of the Big Breakfast house or some other ‘wacky’ late 90s set dressing so I don’t hate it."

Another wrote: ".......I don't hate it." A third said: "I mean it's one way to guarantee viewings, I mean who doesn't want to see that in person?!"

The listing on Rightmove describes the home, which came onto the market at the start of this month and is being marketed by York-based Ashton Estate Agents, as a "beautifully presented four-bedroom detached home" which "offers the perfect balance of tranquil village living and exceptional convenience".

‌



It continues: "Internally, the home has been finished to an exceptional standard, blending style, comfort, and practicality throughout. To the front elevation, a generous lounge provides a welcoming retreat, complete with a charming log-burner - perfect for cosy evenings.

"To the rear, the property has been thoughtfully extended to create an impressive and expansive family room, ideal for modern living and entertaining. This stunning space flows seamlessly into a fully equipped kitchen, designed with both functionality and style in mind.

Article continues below

"The extended accommodation further benefits from a second kitchen, a separate utility room, and a luxurious downstairs sauna - creating a unique and versatile living arrangement. A distinctively designed ground floor WC adds a further touch of character to this remarkable home."

It goes on to describe the bedrooms upstairs, one of which features a Japanese soaking bath, as well as the garden room, or workshop, at the rear. It concludes: "This exceptional home combines generous living space, high-quality finishes, and a highly sought-after location. Offered for immediate viewing, this is a rare opportunity not to be missed."

While AstroTurf is often used to refer to all kinds of artificial grass, it is actually the name of an American company producing artificial turf.

News.title: Sarah Ferguson's £1.5m book deal fail as she's accused of 'cashing in on Epstein scandal'
News.content:
Sarah Ferguson's £1.5m book deal fail as she's accused of 'cashing in on Epstein scandal'

Insiders claim Sarah Ferguson had a US book deal worth £1.5million turned down in the wake of the Epstein scandal - threatening her 'American dream' after previously enjoying success in the country

View 2 Images Sarah Ferguson previously enjoyed success in the US book market (Image: Getty Images)

Sarah Ferguson has been accused of trying to 'cash in on the Epstein scandal' after reportedly having a £1.5million book deal turned down.

‌



Hollywood insiders claim Fergie recently failed to land a lucrative book deal in the US, with major publishing houses appearing uninterested in the former Duchess of York's plans to write a tell-all memoir.

‌



The 66-year-old had reportedly been hoping to secure an advance "in the region of $2million" (£1.49m) - leading to claims she is trying to make money out of the scandal surrounding her and ex-husband Andrew's ties with late paedophile financier Jeffrey Epstein.

‌



View 2 Images The 66-year-old former Duchess also made regular appearances on US TV before the Epstein scandal (Image: ABC via Getty Images)

The source told the Sunday Express: "She ended up with a string of rejections and zero response in a few cases.

"It seems no one is remotely interested in being seen to be enabling her to cash in on the Epstein scandal that has torn her family apart and ruined her ex-husband."

‌



"Any notion she might have had about keeping her American dream alive now seems to have been killed off completely.

"Commercially, as the failed book deal shows, she has become a pariah."

The Mirror has approached her US representatives for comment.

‌



Sarah Ferguson previously enjoyed a successful career stateside as a media personality, including work for NBC’s Today show, appearances on The View and TV adverts for Weight Watchers.

She also authored a number of children's books, including the Budgie the Little Helicopter series and picture book Tea for Ruby.

But royal author Ingrid Seward, who first met Fergie more than 40 years ago, recently told the Mirror she was not surprised by the mention of the ex-Duchess's financial dealings in recently-released Epstein files because of her "extravagant" lifestyle.

Article continues below

She said: "She has a massively endearing type of personality. It's a tragedy that her bad side has got the stronger hold over her than her good side.

"So the bad side is the greed, the stupidity and obsessively wanting things. It’s almost like a shopaholic.... Royal Lodge was always absolutely full of the most beautiful flower arrangements, and we all know what that costs..

"During Covid we saw a lot of Royal Lodge and I thought, 'good heavens, every room has got massive flower arrangements'. So her lifestyle was just so extravagant."

News.title: PM is standing up for UK interests, says Cooper after Trump criticism
News.content: The foreign secretary said "there are some people in politics who think we should just unquestioningly agree with the US whatever" while others "think we should never agree with the US" or "go along with joint action" but that neither was the right approach.

News.title: 'Our children paid the ultimate price': The school shooting that changed Britain
News.content: "You can't meet all those demands but this demand in this way was something, it wasn't about money, it wasn't about priorities, it was just about a terrible, terrible event that the country was determined should lead to a change."

News.title: Hamnet star Jessie Buckley says acting helped her overcome eating disorder as a teenager
News.content: "I didn't know how to be alive the way I wanted to be, and it was difficult," she said. "But I do not for a second regret it, and I think I've been able to transform it and recognise our vulnerabilities as humans in the world.

News.content: "Nothing's come of it. It's kind of leaving this hopeless feeling, where you almost just think you're gonna check every day and never see anything," Long, the content creator, said. "I really don't think we're ever gonna see anything. So it's transferred from that obsession to like a hopelessness."

News.title: Nicky Campbell: How I've learned that certainty is the thing to really fear
News.content: Brexit was certainly an inflection point. On 5Live we had a beeline to the decision makers (in other words, the electorate). Some days, after two hours of fire and fury, I'd feel like ringing Gwyneth Paltrow and getting the name of a good wellness retreat. It was over that time I realised that whatever the person on the other side of the debate said, it was no more than white noise. And it could get bitterly personal and utterly vile very quickly. "People like you" was a phrase I heard a lot, from both sides.

News.title: In maps: Nine days of strikes across the Middle East
News.content: In Minab, in the south of Iran, at least 168 people including children were killed at a girls' school when it was hit on 28 February, Iranian authorities said. Two buildings can be seen in satellite imagery - an Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps base has been completely flattened and the school building has partially collapsed.

News.title: Watch: 'This is a moment of grave peril', says UN humanitarian chief
News.content:
UN humanitarian chief Tom Fletcher has warned that the war in Iran and wider region is having a "massive impact" on civilians, describing it as "a moment of grave, grave peril".

He also voiced concerns about "secondary impacts" of the violence, saying the conflict risked fuelling an increase in extremism and polarisation in the Middle East and beyond.

"We've got to step back from the brink right now", he told the BBC's Laura Kuenssberg.

News.title: UK opens charter flights booking portal for Britons in Dubai
News.content: The BBC's Nick Beake, who was at Dubai Airport on Friday morning, said it felt calm and quiet on Friday, with a steady stream of passengers arriving. He added that there was no visible gathering of expats trying to leave.

Boxing.title: Amir Khan heads to Africa again as boxing promoters battle for control of the sport
Boxing.content:
Promoter Amir Khan is staging another boxing card in Africa, positioning himself in a rapidly shifting promotional landscape where global power brokers are increasingly battling for control of the sport.

Khan's latest event, set for Lagos, Nigeria, blends professional bouts with influencer boxing and regional rivalries - highlighting their strategy aimed at tapping into Africa's large youth audience and social media culture.

The card is topped by a crossover bout between Nigerian online personalities Carter Efe and Portable, who together boast around 10million followers and will settle a long-running online feud in the ring. It is being billed as one of the first major influencer boxing contests staged on the continent.

The event arrives at a time when boxing's promotional structure is undergoing significant upheaval. Frank Warren has been involved in a legal dispute connected to the Saudis, while rival promoter Eddie Hearn has also been navigating the growing influence of Dana White's new venture with TKO, Zuffa Boxing.

Against that backdrop, Khan has increasingly looked toward emerging markets rather than competing directly for the biggest heavyweight spectacles in the Middle East or Las Vegas.

His shows in Africa have mixed international fighters with local prospects and internet personalities, aiming to build audiences in regions where professional boxing infrastructure has historically been limited.

Amir Khan's vision for African boxing is moving faster than most realise. Behind the scenes, AK Promotions is already planning to stage four to five shows per year across the continent

October 2025 saw DAZN-broadcast 'Chaos in the Ring' card in Lagos, co-promoted with Dr. Ezekiel Adamu's Balmoral Group Promotions

The likes of Moses Itauma, David Adeleye and Lawrence Okolie all watched the event live

On the sporting side, the Lagos card features several all-African match-ups. Nigerian cruiserweight puncher Ezra Arenyeka (15-2, 12 KOs), known as the 'Nigerian King,' faces compatriot Godday Appah (14-2, 13 KOs) in a bout between two heavy-handed fighters from rival neighbourhoods in southern Nigeria. Both bring knockout ratios above 80 per cent, adding local rivalry to what organisers expect to be one of the night's most intense fights.

Ghana's Elvis Ahorgah (15-4, 13 KOs), who previously fought British contenders Callum Simpson and Tyler Denny in the UK, returns to Africa to face Newcastle's Joe Laws (15-4, 5 KOs) in a super-middleweight contest.

Elsewhere on the card, British southpaw Michael McKinson (27-2, 4 KOs) takes on unbeaten Algerian prospect Mohammad Sahnoun (8-0, 6 KOs), while teenage Nigerian prodigy Raheem Animashaun (19-0, 12 KOs) meets Tanzania's experienced Emmanuel Amos (22-10-1, 13 KOs) in a youth-versus-veteran clash.

Additional bouts include Nigerian prospect Basit Adebayo against Tanzanian veteran Loren Japhet, national middleweight champion Rasheed Adeyemo facing Nicolaus Michael Mdoe, and UK-based Ghanaian welterweight Samuel Antwi meeting Congolese fighter Paul Kamanga.

For Khan, the card reflects an attempt to carve out a niche as boxing's promotional map shifts. While Saudi Arabia has drawn many of the sport's biggest names and purses, emerging markets such as Africa offer promoters a different growth opportunity.

Boxing.title: Inside the shocking twist that has rocked combat sports: Eddie Hearn and Tom Aspinall tell CHARLOTTE DALY why they have joined forces just days after Conor Benn's stunning defection to Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
Eddie Hearn insists this one isn't about revenge.

But sitting across from the Matchroom boss and the reigning UFC heavyweight champion Tom Aspinall, it is impossible to ignore the wider context.

Matchroom has spent more than four decades building sporting empires, from boxing to darts and snooker, yet the promoter is now embarking on something new.

Matchroom Talent Agency, a fresh subsidiary within the wider Matchroom Sport umbrella, has been created to represent athletes beyond the confines of their sport.

Commercial deals, media opportunities, publishing, digital, broadcast, legal support, fight purse negotiations - the whole ecosystem that surrounds a modern superstar.

And Aspinall, the Salford-born heavyweight king of the UFC, is the first name on the roster - signing a two-year deal with Matchroom Talent Agency.

'It's a two-year deal, a representation agreement that covers every aspect of what Tom does,' Hearn tells Daily Mail Sport. 'On the commercial side, we'll be building his profile, bringing new brands to the table across PR, media, social and digital. But we'll also support the fight side of things.

'This isn't about us stepping in and saying, "Right Tom, we'll go talk to Dana about your career." Andy Aspinall has handled that throughout Tom's career and he'll continue to do so, with our guidance and support. We'll handle the contractual side, our lawyers will review the deals, look at what's next, which fights are on the table, what the purses look like, and make sure everything stacks up.

Tom Aspinall became the first athlete to sign for Matchroom's new talent agency on Thursday

Aspinall hinted at an announcement hours before it was released via an Instagram post

'If Andy is the one having those conversations and we all agree that's the right approach. I'm perfectly happy with that. I don't necessarily need to sit in a room with Dana and negotiate Tom's next fight. But if Tom wants me there, and everyone feels I'm the right person to do it, then of course I'll be there, dealing with Hunter or whoever it needs to be.
